hard.next_step: 6/22 - BD team is maintaining Engagement with Daehwan till they are ready for a serious eval Prashant Saxena Send the follow-up email to Daehwan Kim with Showpad/demo links covering sourcing, contract management, supplier onboarding, and purchasing workflows. Propose a solution consultant-led demo as the next step to walk through real workflows and answer process-level questions. Steve Ovadje Create the Showpad package with relevant demo recordings, case studies, and practical workflow examples. Daehwan Kim Review the demo materials and practical examples shared by Prashant. Confirm which workflow is most relevant for deeper discussion: sourcing, contract management, supplier onboarding, or purchasing.
hard.ais_why:
• At Nellson the 'Merlin Agentic Platform was discussed... presented as an AI-native Source-to-Pay platform,' with the prospect showing interest 'particularly in the AI-driven capabilities such as autonomous negotiation and auctioning functions.'
• The Agentic-platform-discussed flag is set to Yes and evaluator Daehwan Kim has a 'strong need identified around automation, reducing human error' and manual process improvement.
• A post-review demo was requested, indicating the AI-native positioning resonated, though timeline and budget specifics are still forming.
hard.primary_competitor: Status quo / do nothing (manual procurement, no S2P platform)

north_star_verdict.headline: Genuinely interested early Qualified deal off one 03 Jun discovery call where evaluator Daehwan Kim confirmed manual-procurement pain, but engagement is thinning: he ended that call frustrated by generic answers and has not replied to the 22 Jun Showpad follow-up (silent ~33 days as of 06 Jul). Single-threaded on a Purchasing Manager with no economic buyer mapped, no quantified business case, and a 31 Oct close date that is implausible for a deal still pre-demo.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 04 Jun 2026, Qualified 09 Jun, close 31 Oct 2026. Days to close: 117. Time in Qualified: ~27 days. Last two-way buyer engagement: discovery call 03 Jun (~33 days ago). Rep-initiated Next Step 22 Jun awaits buyer reply (rep-side touch, not two-way). No forward slip in field history yet. Typical enterprise S2P motion is 12-15 months; a deal still awaiting its first real demo cannot pass demo, RFI/RFP, shortlist, commercials, EB approval and contracting by 31 Oct. Estimated slip 6-9 months.
evidence: Discovery call 03 Jun 2026 (~33 days ago): Daehwan Kim (Purchasing Manager) articulated operational pain and repeatedly asked to see how the platform works in real life, ending the call early and frustrated that answers stayed generic. He confirmed interest in sourcing, contract management, and purchasing but has not re-engaged since. (source: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026)

Rep-initiated Next Step 22 Jun 2026: BD team to send Showpad/demo links and propose a solution-consultant-led demo, with Daehwan to review and pick a workflow. This is a rep-side touch awaiting a buyer reply; no buyer response is recorded as of 06 Jul. (source: SF Next_Step__c 22 Jun 2026)

Single-threaded: the only opportunity contact role is Daehwan Kim, Evaluator. No economic buyer, decision maker, or executive sponsor is engaged; Daehwan said it is his job to evaluate software and present it to his hierarchy, but that hierarchy is unnamed. (source: SF OpportunityContactRole; Avoma 03 Jun 2026)

No quantified business case: Daehwan named manual pain (human error in ordering, no contract-utilization tracking) but no savings target, error rate, cycle-time, or budget figure. Justifying a $100k spend upward without numbers is hard. (source: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026)

Close date 31 Oct 2026 (117 days) is not credible for a still-pre-demo Qualified deal that must clear demo, shortlist, commercials, EB approval, and contracting. (inference from stage + days-to-close vs typical 12-15 month cycle)

metrics.narrative: Daehwan Kim (Purchasing Manager) described real operational pain on the 03 Jun 2026 discovery call: a JD Edwards MRP signals when to place orders but execution, supplier onboarding, and contract management are all manual, and 'there's a lot of human error could happen.' He called weak contract follow-up 'one of the our company's weakness,' saying 'nobody was really following up the contract' and they only review contract volume 'as a manual when we need it,' so they cannot track contract utilization. However nothing is quantified: no error rate, no FTE hours, no leakage figure, no savings target. He explicitly asked for measurable before/after numbers, which is the wedge for a business case. The next move is to quantify the cost of the manual baseline so a defensible ROI exists to carry upward.
sources: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: 'the problem I always see is that there's a lot of human error could happen. So I was very interested about any kind of automation.'
Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: 'nobody was really following up the contract... we are reviewing as a manual when we need it.'

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified or engaged. The sole opportunity contact is Daehwan Kim, a Purchasing Manager in an Evaluator role, who said on 03 Jun 2026 that it is his job to evaluate software and 'present them to his hierarchy' but never named that hierarchy, a budget owner, or an approval path. No budget figure, funding source, or approval chain surfaced in any field or on the call. The evidence shows only that Daehwan is a gatekeeper-evaluator, not a budget holder for a $100k platform. Identifying and reaching the person who owns the procurement-transformation budget is a ranked move before this deal can advance past interest.
sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Daehwan Kim, Purchasing Manager, Evaluator (only contact role).
Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Prashant Saxena debrief: 'it's his job to evaluate softwares and sort of present them to his hierarchy.'

decision_criteria.narrative: Daehwan's evaluation criteria are practical-proof-led, not feature-led. Across the 03 Jun 2026 call he repeatedly rejected capability descriptions and demanded 'a real life example... how the system is actually work in real life,' pushing for a concrete before/after scenario ('a $10,000,000 contract... 50 supplier that your system can pull up, and then it was automated to compare the price'). He anchored relevance to his own role: 'I'm purchasing manager. So I care about the sourcing contract and then also the actual purchasing process.' So the confirmed criteria are demonstrable, role-relevant workflows in sourcing, contract management, and purchasing, backed by hard numbers; a formal weighted requirements list is not yet established. Meeting these criteria on the next touch is what converts interest into a live evaluation.
sources: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: 'I want to know a example that actually let me understand how the system is actually work in real life.'
Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: 'I'm purchasing manager. So I care about the sourcing contract and then also the actual purchasing process.'

decision_process.narrative: The buying process beyond Daehwan is unknown. The 03 Jun 2026 call revealed only that Daehwan evaluates and then presents upward to an unnamed hierarchy; no committee, evaluation stages, timeline, or approval sequence was described, and the internal Opportunity Health tasks on 10 Jun explicitly flag 'Get clarity on the evaluation stages and influence the process' and 'Decision Maker is not identified' as open. There is no RFI/RFP, shortlist, or workshop cadence in evidence, consistent with a very early qualification. Mapping the evaluation stages and the person who signs off is the process gap to close next.
sources: SF Task 10 Jun 2026: 'Get clarity on the evaluation stages and influence the process.'
SF Task 10 Jun 2026: 'Decision Maker is not identified.'

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics exist yet, which is expected at Qualified stage. There is no evidence of MSA/SOW discussion, procurement channel, legal or InfoSec involvement, or a contract-expiration forcing function, because the deal has not reached commercials. Nellson has no incumbent S2P contract to displace (they run manual processes on JD Edwards), so there is no renewal date to leverage as a clock. Paper-process visibility will only be created once a demo lands and commercials begin; nothing to act on here now.
sources: SF stage Qualified; no procurement/legal/InfoSec signal in any call, task, or field as of 06 Jul 2026.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is confirmed and owned by Daehwan Kim and his corporate purchasing team. On 03 Jun 2026 he described a fully manual procure-to-contract flow: category managers manage suppliers and contracts by hand, human error in ordering is a recurring risk, and there is no systematic contract follow-up so they discover expiring or exhausted contract volume only 'as a manual when we need it.' He framed automation as his motivation for the call: 'I was very interested about any kind of automation.' The pain is genuine but currently qualitative; its owner is the purchasing function Daehwan runs, which makes him the right person to sell up if the value is quantified for him.
sources: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: 'everything was managed by manager... a lot of human error could happen.'
Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: 'nobody was really following up the contract... that's what is happening right now.'

champion.narrative: Daehwan Kim is a hopeful-but-unproven champion, best read as a developing champion at risk. In their post-call debrief Steve Ovadje and Prashant Saxena judged 'what we have is an evaluator and champion here if we can sort of communicate with him in the right way,' noting he is 'excited and quite optimistic about AI and automation' and re-initiated contact himself after a February touch. What is developed: genuine personal interest and a role that requires him to present tools upward. What is missing: any advocacy behavior, no introduction to a budget owner or peers, and no re-engagement since he ended the 03 Jun call frustrated by generic answers. His advocacy is unproven and cooling, so securing him with the concrete proof he asked for ranks above any commercial step.
sources: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026 debrief, Prashant Saxena: 'I think what we have is an evaluator and champion here if we can sort of communicate with him in the right way.'
Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim ended the call early: 'I gotta go... prepare that the real life example... and then I will review it.'

competition.narrative: There is no named software rival: no Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP, or Zip appeared in any source. The competition is the status quo, Nellson's manual procurement on JD Edwards MRP with no CLM or sourcing tool, confirmed by Daehwan on 03 Jun 2026 ('everything is manual'). The threat is inertia rather than a competitor: without quantified ROI and a compelling event the deal can stall at 'interesting but not urgent,' and Daehwan's own read is that the pain 'doesn't really causing big problem' most of the time. We win by making the cost of doing nothing concrete (contract leakage, error rework, tail-spend) and landing role-relevant proof, not by out-featuring a rival.
sources: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026, Daehwan Kim: JD Edwards MRP, no CLM, contracts managed manually by category managers.

deal_movement.summary: Deal is drifting after a single discovery call. Created 04 Jun, moved to Qualified 09 Jun, one discovery call 03 Jun, then rep-side Showpad follow-up planned 22 Jun that still awaits a buyer reply as of 06 Jul. Internal Opportunity Health tasks (10-12 Jun) flag single-threading, no decision maker, and a cold-prospect warning.

items.change: Introductory discovery call held with Daehwan Kim
Ownership transferred from Prashant Saxena to Steve Ovadje
Internal health tasks flag single-thread, no decision maker, prospect cold >14 days
Next Step set: BD team to send Showpad/demo links and propose solution-consultant demo (awaiting buyer reply)

competitive_position.summary: No competing S2P vendor is in play; the single current threat is the status quo of manual procurement on JD Edwards MRP with no CLM, confirmed by Daehwan Kim on 03 Jun 2026. The risk is inertia, not a rival: Daehwan himself downplayed the pain ('doesn't really causing big problem' most of the time), so do-nothing means Nellson keeps managing contracts by hand and never prioritizes a purchase. We win by quantifying the cost of that manual baseline and delivering the role-relevant proof he demanded.

competitors.name: Status quo / do nothing (manual procurement, no S2P platform)
competitors.quote: Daehwan Kim, 03 Jun 2026: 'nobody was really following up the contract... we are reviewing as a manual when we need it,' and on impact, 'it doesn't really causing big problem unless we need to suddenly stop any kind of a contract.'
competitors.how_we_win: Make the cost of manual procurement concrete (contract leakage, ordering-error rework, tail-spend) and land a food & beverage peer proof point with hard numbers, converting a low-urgency nice-to-have into a funded priority.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Daehwan's core expectation is to see the platform prove real, role-relevant value with concrete numbers, not hear capability overviews. Zycus is aligned on domain fit (sourcing, contract, purchasing) but exposed on the proof format he needs, which the 03 Jun call did not deliver.

items.requirement: Show sourcing, contract, and purchasing workflows working in real life (live demo)
items.quote: 'I want to know a example that actually let me understand how the system is actually work in real life.'

items.deliverable: Review the shared demo materials and confirm which workflow (sourcing, contract, supplier onboarding, or purchasing) to go deep on

items.quote: Only contact role is Daehwan Kim (Evaluator); he presents to an unnamed hierarchy.
Internal task: 'Get clarity on the evaluation stages and influence the process.'
Pain articulated qualitatively; no error rate, savings, or cycle-time figure.

best_practice_check.summary: Three levers raise win probability now: multi-thread to a budget owner, quantify the manual-process cost into a business case, and repair the credibility gap from the 03 Jun call with concrete peer proof.
flags: Multi-thread beyond Daehwan to Nellson's procurement leadership / budget owner: a single evaluator with no budget authority cannot fund a $100k platform.
Turn qualitative pain into a business case: quantify manual-process cost (ordering-error rework, contract leakage, tail-spend) so Daehwan can sell upward.
Repair the 03 Jun credibility gap: lead with a food & beverage peer (e.g. Danone) showing hard before/after numbers, since Daehwan felt the first call stayed generic.

items.name: Daehwan Kim
items.title: Purchasing Manager
items.risk: Single point of contact with no budget authority; if the requested proof does not land or he cannot sell upward, the deal stalls at evaluator level.
items.sentiment: Interested but frustrated: articulated manual-procurement pain and optimism about AI/automation, yet ended the 03 Jun call dissatisfied with generic answers and has not replied to the 22 Jun follow-up.

champion_strength.summary: Champion strength is weak. Daehwan Kim shows genuine personal interest and a role that requires presenting tools upward, and the rep debrief hoped he could be 'an evaluator and champion,' but he has demonstrated no advocacy: no intro to a budget owner or peers, no internal selling in evidence, and no re-engagement since he ended the 03 Jun 2026 call frustrated. He is a developing, at-risk champion whose interest is cooling and must be re-earned with the concrete proof he asked for.
champion_strength.source: Avoma discovery call 03 Jun 2026; SF Next_Step__c 22 Jun 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The AI-native / Merlin agentic story (autonomous negotiation, Merlin intake in Teams, supplier risk) was presented and drew Daehwan's interest, but it did not land because he wanted concrete workflow proof, not capability narration. The AI value is under-translated to his day-to-day, so it is currently under-positioned.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS field reads 7.0 / Moderate, driven by Daehwan's stated appetite for automation to cut human error, matching the call where he said 'I was very interested about any kind of automation.' The calls neither over- nor under-rate this: interest is real but he is skeptical and wants proof, so curiosity rather than conviction.

items.detail: Single-threaded on one Purchasing Manager (Evaluator); no economic buyer, decision maker, or executive sponsor engaged.
No quantified business case or budget owner; a $100k spend cannot be justified upward on qualitative pain alone.
Do-nothing risk: Daehwan downplayed the pain as usually not a 'big problem,' so inertia could keep Nellson on manual processes and stall the deal.
Close date 31 Oct 2026 is implausible for a still-pre-demo Qualified deal; date will slip 6-9 months.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling: no two-way buyer contact since the 03 Jun discovery call, the buyer ended that call frustrated, and the 22 Jun rep follow-up remains unanswered.
items: No buyer reply to the 22 Jun 2026 Showpad follow-up as of 06 Jul 2026.
Internal health task 10 Jun 2026 flagged 'Prospect's gone cold? No inbound since last 14 days.'

deal_scores_evidence.summary: An early, single-threaded Qualified deal riding on one evaluator, Daehwan Kim, who confirmed manual-procurement pain on 03 Jun but left frustrated by generic answers and has gone quiet; the one thing between us and momentum is landing the concrete, quantified proof he demanded and reaching a budget owner.

win_position.text: Position is thin and early: interest is real (Daehwan called manual procurement a company weakness and wants automation on 03 Jun) but it rests on a single Purchasing Manager with no budget authority and no economic buyer mapped.
Credibility took a hit on the 03 Jun call: Daehwan repeatedly asked for a real-life quantified example and got capability overviews instead, ending the call early and unconvinced.
Do-nothing is the live threat: Daehwan himself said the manual gap 'doesn't really causing big problem' most of the time, so without a quantified cost of inaction the deal can stall at 'interesting.'

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is stalling: no two-way buyer contact since the 03 Jun discovery call, and the 22 Jun Showpad follow-up still awaits a reply as of 06 Jul.
Engagement is still a single generic intro call with no demo booked and no customer-named dates or next meeting requested.

customer_commitment.text: Commitment is passive: Daehwan agreed only to review materials Zycus sends, made no forward commitment, and has not re-engaged since 03 Jun.
He did re-initiate contact himself after a February touch and asked Zycus to reconnect in June, showing baseline pull that can be rebuilt with the right proof.

deal_risk.text: Single-thread and unmapped power: only Daehwan is engaged and the decision maker is explicitly flagged 'not identified' (internal task 10 Jun).
The 31 Oct 2026 close date is not credible for a pre-demo deal, so the forecast date will slip several months.

champion_strength.evidence: Daehwan is a hopeful but unproven, cooling evaluator-champion with no advocacy behavior shown (Avoma 03 Jun 2026).

customer_preference.evidence: Buyer asked only to be sent information and left the call unconvinced by generic answers (Avoma 03 Jun 2026).

momentum.evidence: No buyer reply since 03 Jun; 22 Jun rep follow-up unanswered (SF Next_Step__c).

scope_change.detail: New-business deal created 04 Jun 2026 with S2C scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, iRisk); no prior scope to compare, no narrowing observed.

critical_signals.text: On the 03 Jun discovery call Daehwan Kim repeatedly demanded a real-life quantified example, got capability overviews, and left frustrated though still interested.
Only Daehwan Kim, a Purchasing Manager with no budget authority, is engaged; the person who owns the procurement budget is unknown and unmet.
No rival vendor is in play; the fight is against Nellson's manual processes on JD Edwards MRP, and Daehwan downplays the pain, so inertia is the real risk.
A $100k platform is on the table with no quantified ROI, no business case, and no confirmed budget owner to justify the spend.

customer_preference.evidence: Daehwan asked only to be sent a real-life example to review ('Share it to me, and then I will review it') and ended the 03 Jun call unconvinced by generic answers; no Zycus-specific preference or next-step pull.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain confirmed (manual procurement, human error, no contract-utilization tracking) but no savings, error-rate, or cycle-time numbers quantified (Avoma 03 Jun 2026).

items.action: Send Daehwan the food & beverage ROI case study and workflow demo videos he requested.
items.trigger: Daehwan asked for a real-life quantified example and ended the 03 Jun call unconvinced.
items.expected_effect: Rebuilds credibility and re-opens a thread that has been silent since 03 Jun, moving toward a live demo.

items.action: Offer Daehwan a 30-minute solution-consultant demo of sourcing, contract, and purchasing workflows.
items.trigger: Buyer wants to see the system 'work in real life' and reps flagged bringing in a solution consultant.
items.expected_effect: Replaces the generic intro with a role-relevant demo, the next milestone to advance past Qualified.

items.action: Ask Daehwan to introduce the procurement leader or budget owner who sponsors this evaluation.
items.trigger: Only Daehwan is engaged; internal task flags 'Decision Maker is not identified.'
items.expected_effect: Maps the economic buyer and breaks single-threading so a $100k spend can be funded.

items.action: Build the tailored use case quantifying Nellson's manual-process cost and savings.
items.trigger: Zycus committed to a tailored use case and pain remains unquantified.
items.expected_effect: Creates a defensible business case Daehwan can carry upward, countering do-nothing inertia.

items.action: Confirm with Daehwan which workflow to go deep on and set the evaluation timeline.
items.trigger: Next Step asks Daehwan to pick a priority workflow; evaluation stages are unclear.
items.expected_effect: Clarifies the decision process and focuses effort on the workflow that matters most to him.

contributions.evidence: Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

commentary.win_position: Win 8 is carried by competitive strength +0.50 (weight 15); business case strength +0.30 (weight 10). Offsetting it: preference strength -0.50 (weight 20).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 47 (roughly flat): Verdict 'Slowing' — momentum stalling. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 14 is driven by Verdict 'Slowing' — momentum stalling. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 0 is anchored on the stage win (8), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 47, and dragged by risk 14. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 3 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.intro: An early, single-threaded Qualified deal riding on one evaluator, Daehwan Kim, who confirmed manual-procurement pain on 03 Jun but left frustrated by generic answers and has gone quiet; the one thing between us and momentum is landing the concrete, quantified proof he demanded and reaching a budget owner
